question: Trace the life cycle of one conditioned association, in order.
items: Acquisition: a tone is repeatedly paired with food until it triggers salivation
The tone is now a conditioned stimulus reliably producing salivation
Extinction: the tone is presented alone many times and salivation fades
A rest period passes with no tone presented at all
Spontaneous recovery: the tone briefly triggers weak salivation again
hints: Learning must be established before it can be extinguished.
Spontaneous recovery can only follow a REST after extinction - it cannot come before extinction.
explanation: Acquisition builds the CS-CR link, extinction weakens it when the CS appears alone, and after a rest spontaneous recovery briefly revives it. That revival, coming AFTER extinction and a delay, is what proves the association was suppressed rather than erased.

question: Classify each scenario by the conditioning process it illustrates.

rows.label: Little Albert, taught to fear a white rat, now also fears a white fuzzy rabbit
A dog salivates to a 1000 Hz tone but learns NOT to salivate to a 1200 Hz tone
After many tone-alone trials, the dog stops salivating to the tone
Weeks later, with no retraining, the tone alone briefly makes the dog salivate again

hints: Ask whether the response is SPREADING to similar cues, NARROWING to one cue, FADING, or briefly RETURNING.
Fearing similar objects is generalization; responding to only one specific cue is discrimination.
explanation: Spreading to similar cues is generalization, narrowing to one cue is discrimination, fading with CS-alone trials is extinction, and a brief unretrained return is spontaneous recovery. Each scenario is placed by what is happening to the response, not by a definition.

question: Sort each described response by whether it shows generalization or discrimination.

items.label: A combat veteran flinches at fireworks, thunder, and any sudden loud bang
A cat comes running only to ITS own can opener, ignoring similar kitchen sounds
A baby scared by one barking dog now cries at every dog
A child learns to fear only the neighbor's specific dog, not other dogs

hints: Generalization SPREADS the response to similar stimuli; discrimination CONFINES it to the exact CS.
'Any loud bang' and 'every dog' signal spreading; 'only its own' and 'only the specific one' signal confining.
explanation: Responding to a whole class of similar cues is generalization; responding only to the precise original cue is discrimination. The giveaway is whether the response has spread outward or been narrowed to one specific stimulus.

question: A student explains extinction. Highlight the one incorrect statement.

tokens.text: Extinction weakens the conditioned response when the CS is presented alone.
Spontaneous recovery shows the response can briefly return after a rest.
Extinction permanently erases the original learned association.
Because the association is suppressed rather than erased, relapse remains a real clinical concern.
Acquisition works best when the neutral stimulus slightly precedes the US.

hints: Four statements are correct; one contradicts what spontaneous recovery demonstrates.
If the association were truly erased, the response could never spontaneously recover.
explanation: The error is t3. Extinction suppresses rather than erases the association - which is exactly why spontaneous recovery (and clinical relapse) can happen. Treating extinction as permanent deletion is the named pitfall of this lesson.

question: Interpret a relapse after therapy.
segments: A therapist successfully extinguishes a client's phobia, yet weeks later the fear briefly returns on its own. This spontaneous recovery shows that extinction
wordBank: suppressed the association rather than erasing it
turned the fear into an unconditioned response
hints: A truly erased association could not come back by itself.
The return of the fear points to suppression, not deletion.
explanation: Spontaneous recovery of the fear shows extinction suppressed the association rather than erasing it - the learning was still there, merely inhibited. This is precisely why relapse after 'extinguished' fears or cravings is a genuine clinical concern.
